items.foco: Definition: intestinal metaplasia in the distal esophagus
items.title: Definition of Barrett
items.contentHtml:
Definition: intestinal metaplasia in the distal esophagus

  • Barrett's esophagus is defined by the presence of intestinal metaplasia in the mucosa of the distal esophagus, identified histologically by goblet cells.
  • It is an acquired condition secondary to chronic gastroesophageal reflux that replaces the squamous epithelium with columnar epithelium specialized.
  • The metaplastic segment must be visible endoscopically and confirmed by biopsy; Without a biopsy there is no diagnosis.
  • The proximal extension from the gastroesophageal junction is key to classify risk.

items.foco: Main risk factor: chronic gastroesophageal reflux
items.title: Main risk factor
items.contentHtml:
Main risk factor: chronic gastroesophageal reflux

  • The main risk factor is chronic gastroesophageal reflux, especially with long-standing symptoms (>5 years) and frequency >3 times/week.
  • Other factors: age >50 years, male sex, Caucasian race, abdominal obesity, hiatal hernia and smoking.
  • The chronic gastroesophageal reflux induces inflammation and epithelial damage, promoting intestinal metaplasia as an adaptive mechanism.
  • The presence of severe erosive esophagitis (Los Angeles grade C/D) increases the risk of Barrett's.

items.title: Endoscopic diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis: endoscopy with confirmatory biopsy

  • Diagnosis requires endoscopy with confirmatory biopsy of the visible columnar mucosa in the distal esophagus.
  • Biopsies should be taken according to the Seattle protocol: 4 quadrants every 1-2 cm of Barrett's segment, and any suspicious area (nodules, ulcers).
  • Barrett is not diagnosed by endoscopic appearance alone; histology is mandatory to document intestinal metaplasia with goblet cells.
  • The use of virtual chromoendoscopy (NBI, FICE) improves the detection of dysplasia and guides biopsies.

items.foco: Prague classification: circumferential and maximum extent
items.title: Prague classification
items.contentHtml:
Prague Classification: circumferential and maximum extension

  • The Prague Classification describes the endoscopic extension of Barrett's by two values: C (circumferential) and M (maximum), in centimeters from the gastroesophageal junction.
  • Example: C2M5 indicates 2 cm of circumferential extension and 5 cm of maximum extension. total.
  • This classification standardizes the description, predicts risk of progression and guides surveillance.
  • A long segment (M ≥3 cm) is associated with a higher risk of dysplasia and adenocarcinoma.

items.title: Association with adenocarcinoma
items.contentHtml:
Association with esophageal adenocarcinoma

  • Barrett's esophagus is the main precursor lesion of esophageal adenocarcinoma, with an annual incidence of 0.1-0.5% in patients without dysplasia.
  • The progression follows the sequence: intestinal metaplasia → low-grade dysplasia → high-grade dysplasia → invasive adenocarcinoma.
  • Factors that increase the risk of progression: dysplasia, male sex, obesity, smoking, long segment and uncontrolled reflux.
  • esophageal adenocarcinoma has a poor prognosis if diagnosed in an advanced stage, so surveillance is critical.

items.foco: Dysplasia as a key premalignant lesion
items.title: Premalignant dysplasia
items.contentHtml:
Dysplasia as a key premalignant lesion

  • Dysplasia is the neoplastic alteration of the metaplastic epithelium without invasion, classified into low grade and high grade according to histological criteria (nuclear atypia, architecture).
  • It is the key premalignant lesion that determines the risk of progression to adenocarcinoma and therapeutic decisions.
  • Confirmation by a second expert pathologist is recommended for any degree of dysplasia, given the interobserver variability.
  • The presence of high-grade dysplasia confers a risk of adenocarcinoma of 10-20% annually.

items.foco: Endoscopic surveillance every 3-5 years without dysplasia
items.title: Surveillance without dysplasia
items.contentHtml:
Endoscopic surveillance every 3-5 years without dysplasia

  • In patients with Barrett's Esophagus without dysplasia, endoscopic surveillance every 3-5 years with Seattle protocol biopsies is recommended.
  • The interval is based on the low risk of annual progression (0.1-0.5%); if there are comorbidities that limit life expectancy, it can be suspended.
  • Endoscopic surveillance every 3-5 years allows dysplasia to be detected in an early stage for curative intervention.
  • If there is no dysplasia in two consecutive endoscopies, the interval can be spaced.

items.foco: Low-grade dysplasia: surveillance or endoscopic resection
items.title: low grade dysplasia
items.contentHtml:
Low-grade dysplasia: surveillance or endoscopic resection

  • If low-grade dysplasia is confirmed, the options are: endoscopic surveillance every 12 months or endoscopic resection if there is a visible lesion.
  • radiofrequency ablation is indicated if there is persistence of low-grade dysplasia in two consecutive endoscopies, to reduce progression.
  • The risk of progression to high-grade dysplasia or adenocarcinoma is 5-10% annually, justifying intervention.
  • Resection of visible lesions is preferred to rule out occult cancer.

items.foco: High-grade dysplasia: endoscopic resection or esophagectomy
items.title: High grade dysplasia
items.contentHtml:
High-grade dysplasia: endoscopic resection or esophagectomy

  • In high-grade dysplasia, the first-line treatment is endoscopic resection of visible lesions, followed by radiofrequency ablation of the remainder of Barrett's epithelium.
  • esophagectomy is reserved for cases with confirmed invasive cancer or when resection endoscopic surgery is not technically possible.
  • Multifocal or recurrent high-grade dysplasia may require esophagectomy if endoscopic treatment fails.
  • The cure rate with endoscopic therapy is >90% in expert centers.

items.title: PPI medical treatment
items.contentHtml:
Medical treatment: proton pump inhibitors

  • The basic medical treatment is with proton pump inhibitors (PPI) at standard doses, such as omeprazole 20 mg or pantoprazole 40 mg once a day, adjusting according to symptoms.
  • proton pump inhibitors reduce the acidity of the reflux, control symptoms and may reduce the risk of dysplasia progression, although they do not reverse established metaplasia.
  • Double dose of PPI is not routinely recommended; the lowest effective dose to control reflux is preferred.
  • PPI therapy is lifelong in most patients with Barrett's.

items.title: radiofrequency ablation
items.contentHtml:
Radiofrequency ablation for dysplasia

  • Radiofrequency ablation (RFA) is the endoscopic treatment of choice to eradicate Barrett's epithelium with dysplasia, both low and high grade.
  • It is applied by balloon or focal catheter, with a controlled ablation depth (500-1000 μm) to minimize stenosis and perforation.
  • Radiofrequency ablation achieves complete eradication of intestinal metaplasia in >80% of cases, reducing the risk of adenocarcinoma.
  • It is recommended to combine it with endoscopic resection of visible lesions to treat high-grade dysplasia.

items.foco: Obesity and tobacco as progression factors
items.title: Progression factors
items.contentHtml:
Obesity and tobacco as progression factors

  • Obesity (especially abdominal obesity, BMI >30 kg/m²) and tobacco are independent factors that accelerate the progression of Barrett's to dysplasia and adenocarcinoma.
  • Obesity increases intra-abdominal pressure and reflux, in addition to promoting systemic inflammation and resistance insulin.
  • Tobacco induces oxidative damage and mutations in the metaplastic epithelium, doubling the risk of cancer.
  • Smoking cessation and weight loss are key interventions in the comprehensive management of patients with Barrett's.
